title: How to Buy Good Beans and Make Great Coffee
description: Most bad coffee isn't brewed badly — it starts with stale, poorly sourced beans. Learning what to look for when buying and the basics of extraction transforms your morning cup.

steps.title: Buy whole beans, never pre-ground
steps.instruction: Coffee loses 60% of its aromatic compounds within 15 minutes of grinding. Pre-ground coffee in a supermarket has been stale for weeks or months. Whole beans stay fresh much longer because the oils and gases are sealed inside. Grind right before you brew.
steps.tip: A basic burr grinder costs $30-50 and is the single best upgrade for your coffee.

media.caption: Why fresh grinding makes all the difference

steps.title: Look for a roast date — not a best-by date
steps.instruction: Quality roasters print the exact roast date on the bag. Coffee is at peak flavour 7-21 days after roasting. Supermarket coffee only shows 'best by' dates 6-12 months out, meaning it was roasted months ago and is already stale when you buy it.
steps.tip: If there's no roast date on the bag, it's probably not specialty coffee.

steps.title: Buy from local roasters or specialty shops
steps.instruction: Local roasters turn over inventory weekly. Specialty coffee shops stock beans roasted within the past 2 weeks. Online specialty roasters ship within days of roasting. Supermarket shelves sit for months. Freshness trumps brand.
steps.tip: Many roasters offer subscriptions that ship beans right after roasting.

steps.title: Start with medium roasts from single origins
steps.instruction: Medium roasts balance the bean's origin flavour with roast character. Single origins (one farm or region) let you taste distinct flavour profiles — Ethiopian coffees taste like blueberries, Colombian like chocolate. Blends are designed to taste consistent but less interesting.
steps.tip: Ask the roaster what flavour notes to expect — good beans have tasting notes on the bag.

steps.title: Use the right coffee-to-water ratio
steps.instruction: The standard starting point is 1:15 to 1:17 — that's 1 gram of coffee to 15-17 grams of water. For a 250ml cup, use about 15-17g of coffee. Too little coffee = weak and sour. Too much = bitter and overwhelming. Use a kitchen scale, not a scoop.
steps.tip: Once you find your preferred ratio, it works across all brew methods.

steps.title: Use water just off the boil — 90-96°C
steps.instruction: Boiling water (100°C) extracts too aggressively and burns the coffee. Water below 85°C under-extracts, leaving it sour and thin. Boil your kettle and wait 30-45 seconds, or use a thermometer. The sweet spot is around 93°C.
steps.tip: If you don't have a thermometer, 30 seconds off the boil works for most kettles.

steps.title: Match your grind size to your brew method
steps.instruction: Coarse grind (sea salt texture) for French press — long steep time needs big particles. Medium grind (table salt) for pour-over and drip. Fine grind (flour texture) for espresso — short contact time needs more surface area. Wrong grind = wrong extraction.
steps.tip: If coffee tastes sour, grind finer. If it's bitter, grind coarser.

steps.title: Bloom your coffee before full extraction
steps.instruction: Fresh coffee releases CO2 when hot water hits it — this is called 'blooming'. Pour just enough water to saturate the grounds (about 2x the coffee weight) and wait 30-45 seconds. This lets gas escape so water can extract evenly during the full pour.
steps.tip: If your coffee doesn't bubble during blooming, the beans are probably stale.

steps.title: Control your total brew time
steps.instruction: Pour-over should take 2.5-4 minutes total. French press steeps for 4 minutes before plunging. Espresso extracts in 25-35 seconds. Time is the variable that tells you if your grind is correct — too fast means grind finer, too slow means grind coarser.
steps.tip: Use your phone timer every brew until you've dialled in your setup.

steps.title: Store beans airtight in a cool, dark place
steps.instruction: Light, heat, air, and moisture are the enemies of fresh coffee. Keep beans in an opaque, airtight container at room temperature. Don't refrigerate — condensation ruins them. Don't freeze unless you're storing for over a month, and never re-freeze.
steps.tip: Buy only what you'll drink in 2-3 weeks to always have fresh coffee.
